title: The Burn Book Problem
subtitle: Hallucinations, verification, and why polished output still needs receipts.
lesson: What hallucinations are and how to verify names, dates, links, numbers, quotes, and high-stakes claims.
emotionalBeat: First betrayal: AI sounded certain, she caught it, and now she trusts herself more.
oneLineDescription: The one in which AI says something confidently wrong and she learns to ask for receipts before forwarding it to a VP.
episodeDescription: The one in which she asks AI something, gets back a confident lie, catches it, and realizes the tool can have Regina George energy without Regina George receipts.
previously: She learned that prompting is delegation: tell AI what you want, what you really really want.
tryOn: Ask AI for an answer with sources, then verify at least three claims yourself before using it.
mainCharacterEnergy: If the output has no receipts, I am not putting my name on it.
rememberLine: Regina George confidence is only useful when you have receipts.
communityPrompt: What is the most confident wrong AI answer you have seen?

siteLinks.label: Bring a suspicious answer to the Burn Book

reelHooks: AI can have Regina George confidence.
If the output has no receipts, do not forward it.
social.carouselIdea: The Receipts Checklist: what to verify before trusting AI.
social.storyPoll: Have you ever caught AI making something up?
social.captionAngle: AI can be useful and wrong at the same time. That is why judgment still matters.
social.cta: Save this before you put AI output in a deck.
